guide.title: Lesson 5.2 — Protecting Data: Managerial Controls — Teacher Guide
guide.subtitle: Unit 5 · Topic 5.2 · 7 class periods · Anchor: Protecting Sensitive Data
overview: Lesson 5.2, Protecting Data: Managerial Controls and Access Controls, sits in Unit 5 (Securing Applications and Data) and turns from naming application and data vulnerabilities to deciding what protection a given piece of data deserves and how to enforce it. Students learn that the state of data (at rest, in transit, in use) and its classification (PII, PHI, PCI) drive the type and degree of security applied, then move to the managerial policies and the access control models that put those decisions into practice, ending with hands-on Linux permission configuration that pairs with the unit performance task 5A, Protecting Sensitive Data.

This Day 1 file carries the lesson-level quiz and this teacher guide across all four learning objectives in the topic's tested scope: how the state or classification of data impacts the type and degree of security applied (5.2.A); identifying managerial controls such as cryptography and web application security policies (5.2.B); determining an appropriate access control model — RBAC, RuBAC, DAC, MAC/Bell-LaPadula, and the principle of least privilege (5.2.C); and configuring access control settings on a Linux-based system with rwx permissions, the ls -l, getfacl, and chmod commands, and both the numeric and symbolic methods (5.2.D).
los: LO 5.2.A — Explain how the state or classification of data impacts the type and degree of security applied to that data.
LO 5.2.B — Identify managerial controls related to application and data security.
LO 5.2.C — Determine an appropriate access control model to protect applications and data.
LO 5.2.D — Configure access control settings on a Linux-based system.
eks: EK 5.2.A.1 — Organizations implement specific security controls to comply with legal requirements based on the types of data they collect, store, process, and transmit.

EK 5.2.A.2 — Data can be classified by their state. Data at rest are stored on a drive; it is important to protect the physical drive from destruction or theft, and data at rest can also be encrypted so that if an adversary steals it they can't immediately read the data. Data in transit are being sent from one device to another; if transferred over physical media (e.g., cables) it is important to protect the media, and data in transit can also be encrypted so that if an adversary intercepts it they can't immediately read the data. Data in use are being processed by software or a person; access controls can be used to limit who or what has the ability to use data in different ways (e.g., view or edit), and data must be unencrypted to be used.
EK 5.2.A.3 — Organizations often categorize data according to their sensitivity and prioritize a higher degree of security for more sensitive information.

EK 5.2.A.4 — Laws and regulations can require certain types of data to be stored, transmitted, and handled according to specific rules. Personally identifiable information (PII) is any data that allows someone to be identified (e.g., name, signature, phone number, address, biometric data, social security number, date of birth, email address); its protection is covered by many laws, most notably The Privacy Act of 1974 and, for children under 13, the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act of 1998. Protected health information (PHI) is any data related to an individual's health, treatment, or payment for healthcare (e.g., test results, treatment records, hospital records, doctor visit notes); its protection is included in the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996. Payment card information (PCI) is data collected to process payments via cards (e.g., name, account number, expiration date, address, CVV code); its protection is regulated by the Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI-DSS).

EK 5.2.A.5 — Organizations that collect regulated data will label them and have policies that comply with the legal or regulatory requirements for the safe storage, transmission, and handling of these data.

EK 5.2.B.1 — A cryptography policy will describe the acceptable encryption protocols and key parameters for an organization and may include a list of encryption algorithms approved for specific uses, minimum or maximum key lengths, cryptographic key-generation requirements and parameters, and cryptographic key-storage requirements.

EK 5.2.B.2 — A web application security policy will outline the requirements and parameters for testing and mitigating web application vulnerabilities in an organization, and it may include parameters for when an application is subject to a security assessment, timelines for remediating vulnerabilities based on level of risk, and parameters for how an application security assessment is to be carried out (e.g., using specific tools or according to specific frameworks).

EK 5.2.C.1 — Access control enforces which users or applications (called subjects) can access, modify, add, or remove (called operations) which files or applications (called objects). Access control models describe how to determine which subjects have what type of access to which objects.
EK 5.2.C.2 — Role-based access control (RBAC) assigns every subject to a role and defines which roles have which types of access to which objects.

EK 5.2.C.3 — Rule-based access control (RuBAC) checks a set of rules to determine what type of access a subject should have for a specific object and then allows or denies types of access based on the rules. This access control model is typically layered on top of another access control model.

EK 5.2.C.4 — Discretionary access control (DAC) gives individual subjects the ability to set the type of access that other subjects have on objects they own. In DAC models some subjects are designated as administrators or super users, and they have the ability to override the access controls established by other subjects.

EK 5.2.C.5 — Mandatory access control (MAC) follows strict rules for which types of access each subject level has for objects that are above their level, at their level, or below their level. Subject and object levels are assigned by an external administrator.

EK 5.2.C.6 — The Bell-LaPadula model is a MAC model often used by governments and military organizations. The Simple Security Property states that subjects may not read objects above their level; the * (Star) Security Property states that subjects may not write to objects below their level. These rules are summarized as "write up, read down" (WURD).
EK 5.2.C.7 — The principle of least privilege is the idea that entities should be given exactly as much access as they need to perform their function and no more.

EK 5.2.D.1 — Authorization is when an entity is granted permission to have a certain type of access to a resource. Access controls are put in place to control which users have what types of access to which data.

EK 5.2.D.2 — There are three types of access to a file in Linux, always in this order: read (r) allows a user to view the contents of a file, write (w) allows a user to make changes to a file, and execute (x) allows a user to run a binary file such as a program. These are abbreviated rwx; a - symbol indicates the absence of that permission (e.g., r-x is read and execute but not write).

EK 5.2.D.3 — There are three default entities for which permissions are set, always in this order: (1) the file owner, (2) the file group, and (3) all other users. The three sets are displayed with no spaces (e.g., rwxrwxrwx).

EK 5.2.D.4 — To view the current permission settings for a file, use the command ls -l. If there is a + symbol at the end of the permissions, this means other permissions have been set for that file and it can be viewed with the getfacl command.
EK 5.2.D.5 — To modify the permission settings for a file, use the chmod command, which can be used with the numeric method or the symbolic method.

EK 5.2.D.6 — In the numeric method the syntax is chmod ### filename, where each # is one entity (owner, group, other). The permission for each entity is the sum of its access values: 0 = no permissions, 1 = execute, 2 = write, 4 = read. Therefore 3 = write and execute, 5 = read and execute, 6 = read and write, and 7 = read, write, and execute (e.g., chmod 750 test gives the owner read/write/execute, the group read/execute, and others none).

EK 5.2.D.7 — In the symbolic method the syntax is chmod entity +(or -)permission filename. Entities are u = user owner, g = group, o = others, a = all; + adds and - removes; permissions are r = read, w = write, x = execute. Entities and permissions can be combined (e.g., chmod ug+rx testfile adds read and execute for the group and user owner).
materials: Projector / slide deck (teacher · deep-dive version) for each of the seven days.

Printed/Digital Day 1–7 Guided Notes (student copies). Workflow: students fill the notes in live during the slide presentation; anything missed can be completed — or checked — afterward against the lesson page, which carries all the content. The formal answer KEYs stay in this folder and are never posted to the site.
Linux terminal or sandbox (or a permissions worksheet) for the chmod and ls -l practice in 5.2.D.
Printed Lesson 5.2 quiz (student copies) for the final day of the topic.
Unit performance task 5A, Protecting Sensitive Data — students configure access control settings for directories and files.
prereqs: Unit 1 vocabulary of confidentiality, integrity, and availability and the idea of security controls, plus the Unit 5 framing of application and data vulnerabilities from Topic 5.1. Learners should be comfortable with the terms encryption, authentication, and file before classifying data and configuring permissions.

pacing.focus: Data states and how state impacts security (LO 5.2.A)

segments.segment: QOTD + bell ringer (what data does a hospital store, send, and use?)
Guided-notes preview + objectives
Legal-driven controls and the three data states: at rest, in transit, in use (EK 5.2.A.1–A.2)
How each state is protected — physical drive/media, encryption, and access controls (EK 5.2.A.2)
Stop and Think + wrap

pacing.focus: Data classification: sensitivity and regulated data (LO 5.2.A)

segments.segment: QOTD + bell ringer
Categorizing by sensitivity and prioritizing higher security (EK 5.2.A.3)
Regulated data — PII, PHI, PCI — and the laws/standards that protect each (EK 5.2.A.4)
Labeling and compliance policies for regulated data (EK 5.2.A.5)
Stop and Think + wrap

pacing.focus: Managerial controls: cryptography and web application security policies (LO 5.2.B)

segments.segment: QOTD + bell ringer
Cryptography policy: approved algorithms, key lengths, key generation and storage (EK 5.2.B.1)
Web application security policy: assessment triggers, remediation timelines, assessment parameters (EK 5.2.B.2)
Stop and Think + wrap

pacing.focus: Access control fundamentals; RBAC and RuBAC (LO 5.2.C)

segments.segment: QOTD + bell ringer
Subjects, objects, operations, and what an access control model is (EK 5.2.C.1)
Role-based access control (RBAC) with the accountant/payroll example (EK 5.2.C.2)
Rule-based access control (RuBAC) layered on another model, with the working-hours example (EK 5.2.C.3)
Stop and Think + wrap

pacing.focus: DAC, MAC, Bell-LaPadula, and least privilege (LO 5.2.C)

segments.segment: QOTD + bell ringer
Discretionary access control (DAC), owners, and super users (EK 5.2.C.4)
Mandatory access control (MAC) and the Bell-LaPadula WURD properties (EK 5.2.C.5–C.6)
The principle of least privilege (EK 5.2.C.7)
Stop and Think + wrap

pacing.focus: Linux permissions: rwx, entities, ls -l, getfacl, and the numeric method (LO 5.2.D)

segments.segment: QOTD + bell ringer
Authorization and the three access types rwx; the three default entities (EK 5.2.D.1–D.3)
Viewing permissions with ls -l and getfacl; modifying with chmod (EK 5.2.D.4–D.5)
chmod numeric method: 0/1/2/4 values and combined permissions (EK 5.2.D.6)
Stop and Think + wrap

pacing.focus: Linux permissions: the symbolic method and topic review (LO 5.2.D)

segments.segment: QOTD + bell ringer
chmod symbolic method: u/g/o/a, +/-, r/w/x, and combined commands (EK 5.2.D.7)
Numeric vs. symbolic practice on the sandbox/worksheet
Topic review + Lesson 5.2 quiz

misconceptions.heading: Data in use can stay encrypted
misconceptions.myth: Strongly protected data is always encrypted, including while it is being processed.
misconceptions.reality: EK 5.2.A.2 states that data in use are being processed by software or a person and that data must be unencrypted to be used. Encryption protects data at rest and in transit; data in use is protected by access controls that limit who can view or edit it, not by staying encrypted.

misconceptions.heading: RBAC vs. RuBAC
misconceptions.myth: Role-based and rule-based access control are the same thing.
misconceptions.reality: EK 5.2.C.2 defines RBAC as assigning every subject to a role and granting access by role, while EK 5.2.C.3 defines RuBAC as checking a set of rules (such as time of day) to allow or deny access, typically layered on top of another model. RBAC keys on who you are; RuBAC keys on conditions the rules check.

misconceptions.heading: Bell-LaPadula 'write up, read down'
misconceptions.myth: Higher-clearance subjects can freely read and write data at any level below them.
misconceptions.reality: EK 5.2.C.6 states the Simple Security Property forbids reading objects above your level and the Star Property forbids writing to objects below your level — summarized as write up, read down (WURD). A high-level subject may read down but may not write down, which prevents leaking sensitive information to a lower level.

misconceptions.heading: Reading the chmod digits
misconceptions.myth: A chmod number like 7 means a single specific permission you simply memorize.
misconceptions.reality: EK 5.2.D.6 states each digit is the sum of 4 (read) + 2 (write) + 1 (execute), so 7 = read + write + execute, 5 = read + execute, and 6 = read + write. The digits are added, and the three digits map in order to owner, group, and others.

support: Give students a two-column reference: one side lists the three data states (at rest, in transit, in use) with how each is protected, and the other lists the three regulated data types (PII, PHI, PCI) with the law or standard for each, so they can match a scenario to the right row.
Provide a chmod conversion card showing 4 = read, 2 = write, 1 = execute and the sums 3/5/6/7, plus the entity order owner-group-other, before students attempt any numeric-method item.
extension: Have students take a single dataset (e.g., patient records) and write a one-paragraph policy that names its classification, the law that applies, how it is protected in each of the three states, and the access control model they would use to limit who can edit it.
Ask students to translate several permission strings (e.g., r-xr-----) into the equivalent chmod numeric command and the equivalent symbolic command, then explain what each entity can do.

guide.answerKeyNote: Each question's full rationale is on the KEY copy of the quiz (print it with the answer-key option). The short answer key below is pulled straight from the quiz on this page, so it always matches the questions students see.

quiz.title: Lesson 5.2 — Protecting Data: Managerial Controls — Quiz
quiz.instructions: Choose the single best answer. 12 questions, 25 minutes, no notes.

questions.stem: A hospital encrypts patient records on its backup server's hard drive and also locks the server room so the physical drive cannot be stolen. The records are not being sent anywhere or opened by any program right now. In which data state are these records, and why does that protection fit?
options: Data at rest — stored on a drive, protected by securing the physical drive and by encryption so a thief cannot immediately read it
Data in transit — being sent between devices, protected by securing the cables and by encryption against interception
Data in use — being processed by software or a person, protected by access controls that limit viewing or editing
Data in transit — being processed by a person, protected by leaving it encrypted while it is read
questions.rationale: EK 5.2.A.2 defines data at rest as data stored on a drive, where it is important to protect the physical drive from destruction or theft and the data can also be encrypted so a thief cannot immediately read it — exactly the hospital's situation. Option B is data in transit (being sent between devices). Option C is data in use (being processed). Option D mislabels processed data as in transit and wrongly claims data stays encrypted while read.

questions.stem: An analyst opens a spreadsheet so that software can process and display the figures on screen for editing. The CED notes a key constraint about protecting data in this state. Which statement is correct for data in use?
options: It is being sent between devices, so the priority is protecting the physical cables it travels over
It is stored on a drive, so the priority is encrypting the drive against theft
It can remain encrypted the entire time it is processed, so no other control is needed
It is being processed by software or a person and must be unencrypted to be used, so access controls limit who can view or edit it
questions.rationale: EK 5.2.A.2 states data in use are being processed by software or a person, that data must be unencrypted to be used, and that access controls limit who or what can use the data in different ways such as view or edit. Option A describes data in transit, option B describes data at rest, and option C contradicts the CED, which says data must be unencrypted to be used.

questions.stem: A retailer stores customers' names, card account numbers, expiration dates, and CVV codes to process card payments. A compliance officer needs to name the data type and the standard that regulates protecting it. Which pairing is correct?
options: Protected health information (PHI), regulated by the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996
Payment card information (PCI), regulated by the Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI-DSS)
Personally identifiable information (PII), regulated most notably by The Privacy Act of 1974
Protected health information (PHI), regulated by the Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI-DSS)
questions.rationale: EK 5.2.A.4 defines payment card information (PCI) as data collected to process card payments — including name, account number, expiration date, address, and CVV code — and states its protection is regulated by the Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI-DSS). Option A and option D name PHI (health data under HIPAA), and option C names PII under The Privacy Act of 1974 — neither matches card-payment data.

questions.stem: A security manager writes a document that lists which encryption algorithms are approved for specific uses, sets minimum and maximum key lengths, and states how cryptographic keys must be generated and stored. Which managerial control is this?
options: A web application security policy — it sets when applications are assessed and how vulnerabilities are remediated
An access control model — it defines which subjects may perform which operations on which objects
A cryptography policy — it describes acceptable encryption protocols and key parameters such as algorithms, key lengths, and key generation and storage
A data classification scheme — it categorizes data by sensitivity to decide how much protection to apply
questions.rationale: EK 5.2.B.1 states a cryptography policy describes acceptable encryption protocols and key parameters and may include approved encryption algorithms for specific uses, minimum or maximum key lengths, and cryptographic key-generation and key-storage requirements — matching the document exactly. Option A is the web application security policy (EK 5.2.B.2), option B is an access control model (EK 5.2.C.1), and option D is data classification (EK 5.2.A.3), none of which govern encryption key parameters.

questions.stem: An organization adopts a policy stating when an application must undergo a security assessment, the timelines for remediating discovered vulnerabilities based on their level of risk, and which tools and frameworks the assessment must follow. Which managerial control does this describe?
options: A web application security policy — it sets assessment triggers, risk-based remediation timelines, and how assessments are carried out
A cryptography policy — it sets approved algorithms, key lengths, and key generation and storage requirements
The principle of least privilege — it grants entities only as much access as they need and no more
A mandatory access control model — it assigns subject and object levels through an external administrator
questions.rationale: EK 5.2.B.2 states a web application security policy outlines requirements for testing and mitigating web application vulnerabilities and may include parameters for when an application is assessed, remediation timelines based on level of risk, and how the assessment is carried out using specific tools or frameworks — matching the policy described. Option B is the cryptography policy (EK 5.2.B.1), option C is least privilege (EK 5.2.C.7), and option D is MAC (EK 5.2.C.5).

questions.stem: At a firm, every employee is assigned to a job category, and access to the payroll software is granted only to whoever holds the 'accountant' category. Access depends entirely on the category a subject is assigned, not on individual decisions. Which access control model is in use?
options: Discretionary access control (DAC) — owners individually set the access other subjects have on their objects
Role-based access control (RBAC) — subjects are assigned to roles, and roles define which access applies to which objects
Rule-based access control (RuBAC) — a set of rules checks conditions to allow or deny each access attempt
Mandatory access control (MAC) — strict level rules govern access to objects above, at, or below a subject's level
questions.rationale: EK 5.2.C.2 defines role-based access control (RBAC) as assigning every subject to a role and defining which roles have which access to which objects — the CED's own example is the 'accountant' role and the payroll software object. Option A (DAC, EK 5.2.C.4) lets owners set access individually, option C (RuBAC, EK 5.2.C.3) checks condition-based rules, and option D (MAC, EK 5.2.C.5) uses assigned security levels.

questions.stem: Bob creates a file he owns. He gives Alice permission to edit it, gives Frank permission to view it only, and denies everyone else any access. An administrator could still override Bob's choices. Which access control model does this illustrate?
options: Mandatory access control (MAC) — an external administrator assigns each subject and object a fixed level
Role-based access control (RBAC) — access is granted by the role a subject is assigned to
Rule-based access control (RuBAC) — rules check conditions such as time of day to allow or deny access
Discretionary access control (DAC) — owners set the access other subjects have on objects they own, and super users can override
questions.rationale: EK 5.2.C.4 defines discretionary access control (DAC) as letting individual subjects set the type of access other subjects have on objects they own, with administrators or super users able to override those controls — the CED's own Bob/Alice/Frank example. Option A (MAC, EK 5.2.C.5) uses externally assigned levels, option B (RBAC, EK 5.2.C.2) grants by role, and option C (RuBAC, EK 5.2.C.3) checks condition-based rules.

questions.stem: A military system uses the Bell-LaPadula model. A subject cleared at 'Secret' wants to read a 'Top Secret' file and also wants to write into an 'Unclassified' file. Applying the model's two properties, what is allowed?
options: Both actions are allowed, since a higher-cleared subject may freely read and write at any lower or higher level
Reading the Top Secret file is denied and writing to the Unclassified file is denied — write up, read down (WURD)
Reading the Top Secret file is allowed and writing to the Unclassified file is allowed, reversing the model's rules
Both actions are denied, since subjects may never read or write any object at a different level
questions.rationale: EK 5.2.C.6 states the Simple Security Property forbids reading objects above your level (so the Secret subject cannot read Top Secret) and the Star Property forbids writing to objects below your level (so the Secret subject cannot write to Unclassified), summarized as write up, read down (WURD). Option A and option C reverse or ignore the rules, and option D is wrong because reading down and writing up are permitted.

questions.stem: A company already grants access by job role, but it adds a layer that blocks all access to a sensitive database outside local working hours — even for users who are normally authorized. It also gives each account only the access strictly needed for its function. Which two CED ideas are at work?
options: Mandatory access control providing the time block, and discretionary access control providing the minimal access
Role-based access control providing the time block, and the principle of least privilege providing the override
Rule-based access control (RuBAC) layered on the role model to enforce the time block, and the principle of least privilege giving each account only what it needs
Discretionary access control providing the time block, and rule-based access control providing the minimal access
questions.rationale: EK 5.2.C.3 defines rule-based access control (RuBAC) as checking rules to allow or deny access — the working-hours block, even for authorized users — and notes RuBAC is typically layered on top of another model (here the role model). EK 5.2.C.7 defines the principle of least privilege as giving entities exactly as much access as they need and no more. The other options misassign the time-based rule to MAC, RBAC, or DAC.

questions.stem: A Linux file's permissions display as r-xr-----. Reading the three sets in order for owner, group, and others, what access does each entity have?
options: Owner: read and write; group: read and execute; others: execute only
Owner: read, write, and execute; group: read only; others: no access
Owner: read and execute; group: read only; others: no access
Owner: read and execute; group: write only; others: read only
questions.rationale: EK 5.2.D.2 abbreviates the access types as rwx with a - marking an absent permission, and EK 5.2.D.3 states the three sets are owner, group, then others. So r-x is read and execute (no write) for the owner, r-- is read only for the group, and --- is no access for others. The other options misread at least one set of three characters.

questions.stem: An administrator runs chmod 640 report.txt. Using the numeric values 4 = read, 2 = write, 1 = execute and the entity order owner-group-other, what permissions does this set?
options: Owner: read and write; group: read only; others: no access
Owner: read, write, and execute; group: read and execute; others: no access
Owner: read and execute; group: write only; others: read only
Owner: write and execute; group: read only; others: read and write
questions.rationale: EK 5.2.D.6 states each digit is the sum of 4 (read), 2 (write), and 1 (execute), and the three digits map to owner, group, then others. So 6 = 4 + 2 = read and write for the owner, 4 = read only for the group, and 0 = no permissions for others. The other options miscompute at least one digit's sum.

questions.stem: A user needs to add read and execute access for both the group and the user owner of a file named testfile, without changing anything for others. Using the chmod symbolic method, which command does this?
options: chmod a-rx testfile, which removes read and execute from all entities at once
chmod o+rw testfile, which adds read and write for other nongroup users
chmod 750 testfile, which uses the numeric method instead of the requested symbolic method
chmod ug+rx testfile, which adds read and execute for the user owner and the group
questions.rationale: EK 5.2.D.7 gives the symbolic syntax chmod entity +(or -)permission filename, with u = user owner, g = group, + = add, r = read, and x = execute, and uses chmod ug+rx testfile as the exact example for adding read and execute for the group and user owner. Option A removes permissions from all, option B targets others with the wrong permissions, and option C is the numeric method rather than the requested symbolic method.
